speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela Konopka, tape number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people in-- in Lyon were not all unfriendly. It was just exploitation of the worst kind. You know, the refugee that has to clean the floors at 4 o'clock in the morning, and that kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to a family that you had known about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Somebody got me there, I don't know anymore. I have to say that the officials-- and I wished I would remember his name. It was something like Monet, but it wasn't Monet. He became very famous after the war-- he became one of the upper people-- politicians in France-- were most helpful. They knew about us refugees. They helped us to get papers, which was worth gold. [FRENCH], they were called. And they helped us. They were fabulous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these [FRENCH] false papers then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Now, I am-- I'm under my name again. And under the right name, because that was very important. And they-- they knew it was a-- I mean, Jewish or not Jewish, the people they protected, those people. And he helped, because he was in an upper position to get those papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And around what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are still-- I'm going to have to go a little faster. It's still just before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just--it's '38, '39. I will go very fast. I go to another family. They're called Noguez. That name I remember, N-O-G-U-E-Z. They were very upper-nobility. And-- but they were-- I stayed with the mother and the two children, first. And took care of the two children. It's like, now I'm not really doing any underground work. I'm just hidden, in some ways, in that country, and being kept alive, if you want to call it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not hiding. It wasn't really hiding. You lived with that family. And you were just a lady who had-- with their children, you know. They took us everywhere. And I think it will too long, because I can tell how we got with them to the Spanish border. I saw, with my own eyes, Spanish refugees trying to swim the Gulf of Biscay, and being caught in the middle, by the fascists, by the Spanish fascists, and being taken back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, I saw that. But that's-- I mean we can't get into all  of that. Was this at this point in time or was that later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's at this point in time. But remember, meanwhile, Franco has come to power, already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Austria is taken by the Nazis. You know, all this is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went into Spain?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't go into Spain. It was just these people-- this family-- took their children and me near the border of Spain. And we saw that. And-- yeah, that's at that time. I don't know anymore how-- oh, yeah. I have to do this quicker. And I forget, partially. The war breaks out, and Noguez doesn't want to have a refugee there, anymore, which is understandable. It's too dangerous. I go back to-- to Paris, where there are people, you know, from the ISK, and refugee committees and all kinds of things. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are alone at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm alone. But Paul is around, still. We see each other when we get into Paris. But we-- you know, he's also in a terrible position. Anyhow, I-- Oh, I don't know anymore the details. I do know that the father of this family, Noguez, has a little place, in the country, outside of Paris. And he allows me to stay there. And he says, nobody would touch me, because he's one of the upper class. This is the upper class. And-- and people were thinking, certainly, that I'm his mistress. But fortunately, he had another mistress, who came, too. And we became good friends. So this is old stories-- and I don't think I have to go. I am in a little place outside of Paris. It's surrounded with several farmers, and so on. And sometimes, I mix up, a little bit, time right now. So I'm not absolutely sure. The war breaks out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The German planes come over. You hear them. I can-- could, at that time, distinguish which was Allied and which-- but Allied, it's not. I mean it's French and English, you know, that are there. There are other things  there, but I forgot. Maybe I'm getting mixed up a little bit with time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I get-- Noguez can't-- there's a time when he cannot let me stay, in this place anymore, for some reason. This, I don't know anymore. And where do I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, there is-- and that name is usually known in literature that I find from refugees, Erich Levinsky-- L-E-V-I-N-S-K-Y. He was a lawyer in Germany, and had to flee. And was also part of the ISK. And had started a restaurant in Paris. I thought that Erich could take me. But he couldn't. He was afraid-- and I understand that. So there wasn't really much that I could do. But Erich had a brother, who lived in Jouy-- that-- funny how some things I remember-- Jouy-en-Josas, J-O-U-Y hyphen E-N J-O-S-A-S. He was a farmer, and he had a French wife. And they said, all right, I can go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for a while, I stayed with them. I stay in that place. I occasionally can go to Paris, because a refugee committee-- and I don't know which one it was-- had ask me to help, occasionally, to go and help them out a little bit. And for that, I worked kind of for my support, which wasn't very big. What did you need to do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, investigate, which I hated. And I can tell horror stories that I saw. A woman they wanted to check whether she really needs the support. I visited that woman in Paris. She was in an empty room, sitting on the floor, eating bread rinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the question, whether she needed support? She was a refugee. And she had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or a man, I still remember that-- who-- an old man, who got very sick, and they had to take him to the hospital. And when I came to visit, he said, I have some money in that drawer. Please, let me have that. It's the only thing I have. And I said, sure. And I put it in his pocket. When I came back to the refugee, I-- they said, this is incredible. That belonged to us, that-- you know. I said, the man is dying. You'll get it anyhow. But this is his only thing that he feels he's not-- he's still a human being. These were Jewish refugees, I remember that. And he was in a French hospital. And he died. And the money went back to the committee. But this is the kind of thing. It wasn't exactly what I think is good social work. And-- but I felt I could do a little bit, something. I got-- I think it's the first time, in my whole life, that I fainted-- in-- when I was in that village, there, and fell down the steps. There was an adorable-- I have to tell you-- an adorable three-year-old, who was standing at the foot of my bed and was pricking me with-- with a pin-- with a pen-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what are you doing? I'm giving you piqures. She had seen that people who are sick get injections. So I think this is the nice things. But anyhow-- Why did you faint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you faint?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hungry. I just had not to each much. I'm sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you eating at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But, you know, if you got maybe a little bread, somewhere. It was very little. Just, no-- you just didn't have to eat. You know, I mean, so little to eat, it wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then-- I think I told you that the war had already broken out, right? Did I say that? And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Paul and some others were immediately interned by the French.

speaker: interviewee
text: The stupid French, these were they're best friends that they could have. Because they were people who fought-- would have fought the Nazis. No, they put them in camps, called them prestataire camps, P-R-E-S-T-A-T-A-I-R-E. And I don't know, anymore, what it meant. Well, they were kind of fixing things and stuff like that. And I saw him, once, one night, I could go there and visit. So that's about-- but that's-- we were separated almost all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this camp located that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the name anymore. But it's-- it's outside of Paris. And they fixed it up, and then the Germans, later, took it over and made it an internment camp for the French. But-- and I hope I don't mix up time. Paul was in that camp, I was in Paris-- not in Paris, in Jouy-en-Josas. The war got worse. The bombs were falling all around. I learned that you can stand against a wall, and then, hopefully, you'll not get caught. But I still remember that I had to once go to Paris, and I had to go into a bomb shelter when the alarms sounded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a man, who was sitting there and crying-- and crying and crying. And we were, at first, not very sympathetic, because we thought, oh, why would anybody cry, you know, at this time? And then we said, what is it? And he said, I'm from Belgium. And on the road from Belgium, the German planes had killed his wife, his children. And that same day, a bus took me back from-- from Paris to Jouy-en-Josas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was always a farmhouse that we passed. And when this time, when we passed, there was nothing but a hole, and everybody was killed. So it was already pretty serious, what went on there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There's so many things, but I can't remember them well enough. And maybe I can, but I can't right now. Anyhow, one day, I have again to go to Paris, you know, for something with refugees and so on, nothing special. But a friend of mine comes running where I am and says, you and everybody else who is a refugee must immediately leave Paris. You can't even go back to Jouy, because the Germans are invading. And they're coming. They were soon in Paris. The subways are already getting filled. And-- so I go out. And those who know Paris know the Gare Saint-Lazare, L-A-Z-A-R-E. Now, they had completely locked it, so that nobody could get into the-- you know, to get into any train. Just the French had locked it. There were--you can't believe it. There were, I think, millions of people sitting around this place. Meanwhile, not only the bombs came over, but a lot of black soot-- not soot or whatever it is. It's the-- to make the city dark, so that the Germans shouldn't see where they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people were sitting there, full of soot, and black stuff. And sitting around there, waiting, what will happen. I'm not very good at waiting, ever. And I started walking, just walking around. Suddenly, I see there's a hole in that thing that they had there, you know, the enclosure around the train. So I just walked through it. I go in, and there are trains sitting on the-- what do you call it? Track?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tracks. And I ask somebody if the train is going south. And I go in the train. The train is filled with people. In my pocket, I never forget, all I had was a bunch of hazelnuts-- we always had that, in case anything should happen, that we have something to eat-- and a lemon, in case there's no water. So I had that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you buy those or were they given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had those-- that was kind of standard for anyone of us, that we should have those things with us. Even if we didn't have much else to eat, this we had in our pockets. So I remember that, very vividly. So I had that. I got on the train. The train was filled with people. And there we were, going, all kinds-- soldiers and so on. I forgot. I should have said that-- well, let me say that later. It goes down. We were told before, from somebody, that anybody Austrian should go to Montauban-- It's a southern French town, M-O-N-T-A-U-B-A-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have to-- can I retract or something very, very important? Because it happened while I still was in Jouy. And then a telegram came. And I thought, oh, that means I will have to leave. But it wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: The telegram said that Ruth, my beloved sister, had been killed in an accident. She was a nurse. And they were rushing around, and so-- this was about the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wales with a friend. And that friend is Lisa-- Lisa? Einstein. I should know that, because she's a relative of Einstein. Lisa, she's is Israel. But her name is not Einstein now. It was Einstein then. She was married, but later, you know. But Lisa was a very close friend. And Ruth was killed in Wales. So I think I should say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all these things-- Ruth is killed. I don't know. Paul and I had agreed that wherever we are, if we can, we write something for-- what is it called? Poste restante. But that's all. We didn't know whether we would ever see each other again. So I'm on that plane-- plane-- train, going to Montauban, when the first-- in the night-- the train stops, oh, every-- seems every hour or so, because of all these bombs falling and stuff like that. But at one point, the news comes that Italy has entered the war. And we are going south.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we go to Montauban. I really must do this faster, because I will never get to the bitter end, or the good end, whatever it is. We-- in Montauban, the mayor was a socialist and a wonderful person. Everywhere at the walls were signs that said, citizens, when the great floods, when the Tarn was running over-- that's a river, T-A-R-N-- and the Tarn is running over, we got help from everywhere. Now, there is another flood, the Germans overrunning all the good people. We must help them. And that was everywhere. And so-- this-- I'll make that very quick. I think the houses, everywhere, were opened to refugees. And it doesn't matter whether they were Jews or non-- other people who fought the Nazis. The people named, where we stayed, where I stayed and somebody else, I still remember was Rouquette, R-O-U-Q-U-E-T-T-E. And they are dead, now, but we go over  to them later. And so we-- Montauban was a refuge. And we even-- that mayor gave us papers so that we could get some food. It was just extraordinary. This is in the Zone Interdit. That is the Z-O-N-E and then I-N-T-E-R-D-I-T. That means the Germans were not supposed to come there, you know. But they did. They constantly-- we heard those marches all the time. So-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard the marches on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, no. They came, always with battalions, to check out something. They were not supposed to. But Petain allowed everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to remember that there were people in France who were very good collaborators. There were very courageous anti-Nazis in France. But this is not the same. So to make it very quick, it wasn't good that everybody should stay in Montauban. The city got filled up with people. Where did you stay? You said you stayed with the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Rouquettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In their house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They were mail-- he was a mail carrier. Very simple people-- so kind, you can't believe it. You were never hungry when you were there. And then we should go to Lavit. No, that's not true. I have to interrupt. I made a mistake. I had lost every contact with Paul. I thought he was dead. He thought I was dead. But Paul had been in that internment camp. They did something, and I will not go into details, something very strange. They told the people, who interned them, who were French, the Germans are coming. We are not staying here, because all they do is torture us. You give us papers that we can go on the road. And so they typed out their own papers. We have a sauf conduit, S-A-U-F C-O-N-D-U-I-T. We have the right to walk. Paul walked from Montauban, thinking that he could go to Lisbon, to maybe go then to England, to continue the fight. He had no idea where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he is-- and this is quite a story. But if I tell that one, we'll never get through. Because his writing is superb, and he wrote it all down.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, at one point, he is on the road. He lived on nothing but sour-- and literally sour grapes. But at one point, he sees somebody on a bicycle whose back looks familiar. And that is Rene, the one who got us into the underground work when he came first to Chambourg. And they yell at each other, they embrace each other. And Rene says, I know where Gisela is. And I'm, meanwhile, in Montauban. And I think Paul is dead. And somebody says, there is something at poste restante for you. And I go there, and there is Rene's note-- Paul is on the way. So we met, again, in Montauban.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that reunion like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. This is strange. I think there is some things that memory doesn't hold. All I know, we were so completely overwhelmed that we even saw each other again, that I cannot tell you. Because you have to also understand that we never thought we'd come out of this alive. So it's like two dead ones meeting, finding it's wonderful, but not thinking there's any--

speaker: interviewee
text: People always say, did you have hope? No, we didn't have hope. We weren't hopeless, because we always said, we have to do something. But no, I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, we said we'd do something. And they said, Lavit would be better. That's out in the country, a little place. Somebody-- for some reason, we got bicycles. I don't know. And Paul and I, on our bicycles, bicycled to Lavit. Yes, Lavit is a tiny village in France. They always talk about one village, which was wonderful. But I tell you, there were many villages that were wonderful. And in that village, the mayor had decided that he lets refugees come in. He will give them false-- they can get false papers. And they can be, somehow, kept there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, when we got to Lavit, there was that mayor. And he gave us a paper. And what did you want to be? And we were Belgians. Because when you are Belgian-- I spoke perfect French, so that's all right. They speak that in Belgium. Paul spoke very good Plattdeutsch, you know, the northern part of Germany? He could speak that-- which is what they speak in the Flemish part. So we were a very good Belgian couple. And I can't tell you the name. I don't know anymore. So they gave us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would I call it? It's a stable, literally, a stable. We had to crawl in, like an animal. Who gave this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor. Whatever he had, they gave us. That was what they had, a stable. And you crawled in. I know. My legs were not like they are  now. And you crawled in there, and you slept on the straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm doing this fast. We were-- and that was literally hidden in Lavit, because the German police-- or not police. They had no right to do that, but they came all the time, looking where there were people. Did this stable belong to a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I don't know the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we were Belgian refugees. And they took care of Belgian refugees. They were very nice to Belgian refugees. The trouble is that when the Germans were through with Belgium, a lot of Belgians went back. And we stayed. So there was some question. But they were very-- these farmers were wonderful. They were incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work for them? Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's another thing. First, there was no food. And Paul, after years and years, would never touch a blackberry again, because we lived literally on blackberries. They grew wild. And we had those. And we occasionally got some milk. But then, the farmers began to know us, especially since Paul can do anything. So he got things from the garbage stuff, where there were old tires. And he cut them up and made soles, because our soles were, meanwhile, gone. And they saw that, and they needed those, too. They didn't have anything either. So every farmer would come around. Paul would make soles. And then, after that, it got very exciting, because they wanted us to help with the wine harvest. I mean the grape harvest. And we ate chicken. I've never forgotten-- I love chicken. It's the best food in the world. But it was different. We were part of the farm. I learned-- I always knew, very well, how to knit. They gave me old wool, I knit for all the women around there-- pants in green and red and whatever-- because other stuff wasn't there. Another refugee couple was situated not far from where we were. And I can't remember their name. And we could even meet at times. I'm doing this as quick as I can. They were Spanish refugees, very poor. And I remember that little girl. I mean the-- she cried, because her teacher said their feet should be clean. But there was no water. No, there was no soap. But Paul and I-- Paul had brought one piece of soap that we saved. So we washed her feet. And the everything worked. That child, we, later, had lots of contact with her. But, now, I forgot her name. Things go. But, so, this was not idyllic. It was hard. But we were feeling comparatively safe. There came rumors that an American visa might come, you know, a special visa for people who are under special persecution. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For that, you needed your own name. You couldn't go under the names that we were there. So, and for that, you needed a working [FRENCH]. That was amazing. We were born, again, by some way, getting our names back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this through the ISK, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- well, no. This is, meanwhile, the French, some people there that were in that village. But Paul and I thought the best way would be to get married, because then we could get married together, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they had already the banners hung up. But the--how they call it? The big honchos there, who was the lawyer or the top banana, would not marry a Jew and non-Jew.
